Simplicial complexes — combinatorial side

A simplicial complex is, at heart, a hereditary family of finite sets: pick a vertex set, declare which subsets are "filled in", and require that any subset of a filled subset is itself filled. From this almost-trivial axiom flow $f$- and $h$-vectors that count faces, Dehn–Sommerville symmetries on simplicial spheres, shellings that propagate Cohen–Macaulayness, the Stanley–Reisner monomial-ideal bridge to commutative algebra, nerves that recover the topology of an open cover, and persistent homology — the noise-tolerant homology that reads barcodes off a point cloud. This page walks the combinatorial toys; the geometric/topological cousins live on algebraic topology.

blocks.html:
1. Abstract simplicial complexes

An abstract simplicial complex on a finite vertex set $V$ is a collection $K \subseteq 2^V$ such that

  • $\sigma \in K$ and $\tau \subseteq \sigma$ imply $\tau \in K$ (closed under taking subsets), and
  • $\{v\} \in K$ for every $v \in V$ (every vertex is a $0$-face).

An element $\sigma \in K$ of cardinality $k+1$ is a $k$-face (or $k$-simplex): vertices are $0$-faces, edges are $1$-faces, triangles are $2$-faces. The dimension $\dim K = \max_{\sigma \in K}(|\sigma|-1)$. The maximal faces (faces not contained in any larger face) are the facets; $K$ is determined by listing them, since every other face is a subset.

The hereditary axiom — sometimes called the downward-closed or simplicial axiom — is the only thing distinguishing $K$ from an arbitrary set system. It is exactly what lets a $k$-simplex carry a well-defined boundary: the union of its $(k-1)$-faces also lies in $K$.

Two flavors of "simplicial complex". The combinatorial object above is a finite set system with a downward-closure axiom. Its geometric realization $|K| \subset \mathbb{R}^N$ — glue an abstract $k$-simplex for each $k$-face — is a topological space that the algebraic topologists work with. The two are equivalent for the purposes of singular and simplicial homology, but combinatorialists prefer the discrete data; topologists, the space.

Boolean intuition: $K$ vs. its face lattice

Order $K$ by inclusion. The result is a graded poset $(K, \subseteq)$ — the face lattice — sitting inside the Boolean lattice $2^V$. It is closed downward and graded by dimension. Any down-closed subset of a Boolean lattice is the face lattice of a unique simplicial complex on $V$.

Face explorer
click a facet to toggle it; the complex closes downward automatically
Yellow vertices, blue edges, green-filled triangles. The readout lists the faces grouped by dimension and shows the corresponding $f$-vector (tally of $k$-faces by dimension).

/* ============================ widget 1: face explorer ============================ */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#faces-svg'), out = $('#faces-out');
  // vertex coordinates in viewBox
  const pos = [
    [ 90,140],[230, 50],[230,230],[420,230],[420, 50]
  ];
  const initialFacets = new Set(['012','023','13','34']);
  const facetButtons = $$('#w-faces button[data-facet]');
  facetButtons.forEach(b=>{
    if(initialFacets.has(b.dataset.facet)) b.classList.add('active'); else b.classList.remove('active');
    b.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ b.classList.toggle('active'); render(); });
  });

  function chosenFacets(){
    return facetButtons.filter(b=>b.classList.contains('active')).map(b=>b.dataset.facet);
  }
  // close downward: collect all subsets of every facet
  function downwardClose(facets){
    const set = new Set();
    function powerset(s){
      const arr = s.split('');
      const n = arr.length;
      for(let mask=1; mask<(1<<n); mask++){
        const sub = [];
        for(let i=0;i<n;i++) if(mask&(1<<i)) sub.push(arr[i]);
        set.add(sub.sort().join(''));
      }
    }
    for(const f of facets) powerset(f);
    return [...set];
  }
  function render(){
    const facets = chosenFacets();
    const faces = downwardClose(facets);
    // f-vector
    const dims = {};
    for(const f of faces){
      const d = f.length - 1;
      dims[d] = (dims[d]||0)+1;
    }
    const dmax = Math.max(-1, ...Object.keys(dims).map(Number));
    const fvec = [];
    for(let d=-1; d<=dmax; d++){
      if(d===-1){ fvec.push(1); continue; } // empty face
      fvec.push(dims[d]||0);
    }

    svg.innerHTML='';
    // draw filled triangles first
    for(const f of faces){
      if(f.length!==3) continue;
      const ids = f.split('').map(Number);
      const pts = ids.map(i=>pos[i]).map(p=>p.join(',')).join(' ');
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polygon',{points:pts, fill:'var(--green)', 'fill-opacity':0.32, stroke:'none'}));
    }
    // edges
    for(const f of faces){
      if(f.length!==2) continue;
      const [a,b] = f.split('').map(Number);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pos[a][0],y1:pos[a][1],x2:pos[b][0],y2:pos[b][1], stroke:'var(--blue)', 'stroke-width':2}));
    }
    // vertices
    for(let v=0; v<5; v++){
      const inComplex = faces.includes(String(v));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:pos[v][0],cy:pos[v][1], r:10,
        fill: inComplex ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--panel2)',
        stroke:'var(--ink)', 'stroke-width':1.5}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:pos[v][0], y:pos[v][1]-16, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = `v${v}`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }

    // readout
    const groups = {};
    for(const f of faces){
      const d = f.length - 1;
      groups[d] = groups[d]||[];
      groups[d].push('{' + f.split('').join(',') + '}');
    }
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(`facets:  ${facets.map(f=>'{'+f.split('').join(',')+'}').join(', ') || '(empty)'}`);
    lines.push(`dim K  = ${dmax}`);
    for(let d=0; d<=dmax; d++){
      const list = (groups[d]||[]);
      lines.push(`f_${d} = ${list.length}   ${list.join(' ')}`);
    }
    lines.push(`f-vector  = (${fvec.slice(1).join(', ')})    [counting f_0, f_1, ...]`);
    lines.push(`Euler χ = ${fvec.slice(1).reduce((s,x,i)=>s + ((-1)**i)*x, 0)}`);
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }
  render();
})();
</script>

2. The $f$-vector and the $h$-vector

For a $(d-1)$-dimensional simplicial complex $K$, the $f$-vector $$f(K) = (f_{-1}, f_0, f_1, \ldots, f_{d-1}), \qquad f_k = \#\{k\text{-faces of }K\}.$$ We always set $f_{-1} = 1$, counting the empty face. The Euler characteristic is the alternating sum $$\chi(K) = \sum_{k=0}^{d-1} (-1)^k f_k = f_0 - f_1 + f_2 - \cdots,$$ and equals the topological Euler characteristic of the geometric realization $|K|$.

The $h$-vector

Define $h(K) = (h_0, h_1, \ldots, h_d)$ by the polynomial identity $$\sum_{k=0}^{d} h_k\, t^{d-k} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{d} f_{k-1}\, (t-1)^{d-k}.$$ Equivalently, expand and read off coefficients: $$h_k \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{k} (-1)^{k-i}\binom{d-i}{k-i}\, f_{i-1}.$$ The $h$-vector and $f$-vector encode the same data, but the $h$-side often has signs and integers that the $f$-side does not. The total $\sum h_k = f_{d-1}$ (the number of facets when $K$ is pure).

Dehn–Sommerville (simplicial polytopes / spheres). If $K$ is the boundary complex of a simplicial $d$-polytope (or, more generally, a simplicial $(d-1)$-sphere), then $$h_k = h_{d-k}, \qquad k = 0, 1, \ldots, d.$$ This is a strong constraint on $f$-vectors: out of the $d$ free numbers $f_0, \ldots, f_{d-1}$, only roughly $d/2$ are independent. Stanley's $g$-theorem (1980, with McMullen's $g$-conjecture from 1971 as the upper-bound side) classifies which $h$-vectors actually arise.

The bow-tie is not a sphere — its $h$-vector visibly fails $h_k = h_{d-k}$, illustrating that Dehn–Sommerville is a diagnostic for "is this a simplicial sphere?".

/* ============================ widget 2: f to h converter ============================ */
(function(){
  const sel = $('#fh-pick'), out = $('#fh-out');
  // each entry: { d: dim+1 (number of h entries), f: f-vector [f_0, f_1, ...] }
  const data = {
    'bd-tetra': { name:'∂Δ³ (4 vertices, 4 facets)', d:3, f:[4,6,4] },
    'bd-octa':  { name:'∂(octahedron) (6 vertices, 8 facets)', d:3, f:[6,12,8] },
    'bd-icos':  { name:'∂(icosahedron) (12 vertices, 30 edges, 20 facets)', d:3, f:[12,30,20] },
    'bd-4simplex': { name:'∂Δ⁴ (5 vertices, 10 edges, 10 triangles, 5 tets)', d:4, f:[5,10,10,5] },
    'cross-3':  { name:'cross-polytope β₃ boundary (6 vertices, 12 edges, 8 facets)', d:3, f:[6,12,8] },
    'bow-tie':  { name:'bow-tie: two triangles sharing a vertex', d:3, f:[5,6,2] }
  };
  function fToH(d, fvec){
    // f_{-1} = 1 prepended; want h such that sum h_k t^{d-k} = sum f_{i-1} (t-1)^{d-i+1?}
    // Use formula h_k = sum_{i=0..k} (-1)^{k-i} C(d-i, k-i) f_{i-1}, i=0..k where f_{-1}=1.
    const fAug = [1, ...fvec]; // index by i: fAug[i] = f_{i-1}
    const h = [];
    for(let k=0; k<=d; k++){
      let s = 0;
      for(let i=0; i<=k; i++){
        const sign = ((k-i)%2===0)?1:-1;
        const binom = binomial(d - i, k - i);
        s += sign * binom * fAug[i];
      }
      h.push(s);
    }
    return h;
  }
  function binomial(n,k){
    if(k<0||k>n) return 0;
    if(k===0||k===n) return 1;
    let r = 1;
    for(let i=1;i<=k;i++){ r = r*(n-i+1)/i; }
    return Math.round(r);
  }
  function chi(fvec){
    return fvec.reduce((s,x,i)=> s + ((-1)**i)*x, 0);
  }
  function render(){
    const key = sel.value;
    const o = data[key];
    const h = fToH(o.d, o.f);
    const sym = h.every((v,k)=>v===h[o.d-k]);
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(o.name);
    lines.push(`dim K   = ${o.d - 1}`);
    lines.push(`f-vector = (${o.f.join(', ')})       (Euler χ = ${chi(o.f)})`);
    lines.push(`h-vector = (${h.join(', ')})        sum h = ${h.reduce((s,x)=>s+x,0)}`);
    lines.push(`Dehn–Sommerville test  h_k = h_{d-k} : ${sym ? '✓ symmetric (consistent with simplicial sphere)' : '✗ not symmetric — NOT a simplicial sphere'}`);
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }
  sel.addEventListener('change', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

3. Geometric realization and the nerve

The geometric realization of an abstract complex $K$ is the topological space $|K|$ obtained by gluing one standard $k$-simplex $\Delta^k \subset \mathbb{R}^{k+1}$ for each $k$-face along the obvious face inclusions. Combinatorial homology of $K$ — chains generated by faces, boundary $\partial\sigma = \sum (-1)^i \sigma \setminus \{v_i\}$ — agrees with the singular homology of $|K|$. So the combinatorial side computes $H_n(|K|;\mathbb{Z})$.

The other key way simplicial complexes appear in topology is via the nerve construction. Given a finite cover $\mathcal{U} = \{U_1, \ldots, U_n\}$ of a space $X$, the nerve $\mathcal{N}(\mathcal{U})$ is the abstract complex with vertex set $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ and a face $\sigma \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ exactly when $\bigcap_{i\in\sigma} U_i \neq \emptyset$. This is downward-closed by construction (any subintersection of a non-empty intersection is non-empty).

Nerve theorem (Borsuk / Leray). If $\mathcal{U}$ is an open cover by sets each of whose finite intersections is empty or contractible — a so-called good cover — then the nerve $\mathcal{N}(\mathcal{U})$ has the homotopy type of $X$. So combinatorics on the cover recovers topology of the space, and that is what makes simplicial complexes a viable computational substitute for topological spaces.

Toy: nerve of a ball cover of points in the plane

Place a few points $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and let $U_i$ be the disk $B(x_i, r)$ of radius $r$. The nerve $\mathcal{N}(r)$ has $\sigma$ as a face iff the disks indexed by $\sigma$ have a common point. As $r$ grows, more faces appear; at $r=0$ the nerve is just the vertices, and for $r \gg \max d(x_i, x_j)$ it is the full simplex $\Delta^{n-1}$.

/* ============================ widget 3: disk-cover nerve ============================ */
(function(){
  const rIn = $('#nerve-r'), rv = $('#nerve-rv');
  const svg = $('#nerve-svg'), out = $('#nerve-out');
  // 5 points in viewBox top-half (cover area) -- coordinates chosen for interesting topology
  const pts = [
    [120, 80], [240, 50], [340,120], [200,150], [100,170]
  ];
  const N = pts.length;

  function diskIntersect(idxs, r){
    // are the disks at pts[idxs[*]] with radius r jointly non-empty?
    // For 2 disks: distance <= 2r.
    // For 3 disks in R^2: pairwise <= 2r AND the smallest enclosing circle of the centers <= r.
    // For higher: we use a simple-and-correct method — sample a tiny grid in the bounding box.
    if(idxs.length===1) return true;
    if(idxs.length===2){
      const [i,j]=idxs;
      const d = Math.hypot(pts[i][0]-pts[j][0], pts[i][1]-pts[j][1]);
      return d <= 2*r + 1e-6;
    }
    // grid sample
    let xMin=Infinity, xMax=-Infinity, yMin=Infinity, yMax=-Infinity;
    for(const i of idxs){
      xMin = Math.min(xMin, pts[i][0] - r); xMax = Math.max(xMax, pts[i][0] + r);
      yMin = Math.min(yMin, pts[i][1] - r); yMax = Math.max(yMax, pts[i][1] + r);
    }
    const steps = 40;
    for(let a=0; a<=steps; a++) for(let b=0; b<=steps; b++){
      const x = xMin + (xMax-xMin)*a/steps;
      const y = yMin + (yMax-yMin)*b/steps;
      let ok = true;
      for(const i of idxs){
        if(Math.hypot(pts[i][0]-x, pts[i][1]-y) > r){ ok=false; break; }
      }
      if(ok) return true;
    }
    return false;
  }

    }

    svg.appendChild(lblTop);
    const lblBot = SVG('text',{x:310, y:298,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    lblBot.textContent = `nerve  𝒩(𝒰_r) — f-vector (${fv.filter(x=>x>0).join(', ')})`;
    svg.appendChild(lblBot);

4. Shellings and Cohen–Macaulay complexes

Call a pure $(d-1)$-complex $K$ shellable if its facets can be ordered $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_m$ so that for every $i \ge 2$, the intersection $$F_i \cap \bigl(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \cdots \cup F_{i-1}\bigr) \;=\; \text{a pure } (d-2)\text{-subcomplex of } \partial F_i.$$ Geometrically: each new facet glues onto the previous union along a contractible-piece-of-its-boundary that is itself a union of codimension-$1$ faces. Boundaries of simplicial polytopes are shellable (Bruggesser–Mani 1971); shellings power inductive proofs about $h$-vectors.

Why shellability matters

A shelling decomposes the simplicial chain complex into manageable summands and leads directly to:

  • Counting: if $K$ is shellable then $h_k$ counts facets $F_i$ whose restriction $\mathcal{R}(F_i)$ has cardinality $k$ — so $h_k \ge 0$.
  • Homotopy type: a shellable pure $(d-1)$-complex is homotopy-equivalent to a wedge of $(d-1)$-spheres, with the number of spheres equal to $h_d$.
  • Algebra: shellable $\Rightarrow$ Cohen–Macaulay (over any field).

A complex $K$ is Cohen–Macaulay (CM) over $k$ if its Stanley–Reisner ring (next section) is Cohen–Macaulay as a graded $k$-algebra. Reisner's criterion (1976) gives a purely topological characterisation:

Reisner's criterion. $K$ is Cohen–Macaulay over $k$ iff for every face $\sigma \in K$ (including $\sigma = \emptyset$), the reduced homology of the link $$\operatorname{lk}_K(\sigma) \;=\; \{\tau \in K : \sigma \cap \tau = \emptyset,\ \sigma \cup \tau \in K\}$$ satisfies $\widetilde{H}_i(\operatorname{lk}_K(\sigma); k) = 0$ for $i < \dim\operatorname{lk}_K(\sigma)$. Spheres and balls are CM; complexes with "wrong-dimensional holes in some link" are not.

/* ============================ widget 4: shelling order ============================ */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#shell-svg'), out = $('#shell-out');
  const btnReset = $('#shell-reset'), btnBad = $('#shell-test');
  // hexagonal triangulation: vertex 0 = center, vertices 1..6 around
  const cx0=310, cy0=160, R=110;
  const verts = [[cx0,cy0]];
  for(let k=0;k<6;k++){
    const t = -Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/6;
    verts.push([cx0 + R*Math.cos(t), cy0 + R*Math.sin(t)]);
  }
  // 6 triangle facets
  const facets = [
    [0,1,2],[0,2,3],[0,3,4],[0,4,5],[0,5,6],[0,6,1]
  ];
  let order = []; // chosen indices
  let badMode = false;

  function edgesOf(facet){
    const [a,b,c] = facet;
    return [[a,b],[a,c],[b,c]].map(e=>e.sort((x,y)=>x-y).join('-'));
  }
  function unionOfFirst(k){
    const eset = new Set();
    for(let i=0;i<k;i++){
      for(const e of edgesOf(facets[order[i]])) eset.add(e);
    }
    return eset;
  }
  function isShellingExtension(idx){
    if(order.length===0) return true;
    const prev = unionOfFirst(order.length);
    const newFacet = facets[idx];
    // intersection of newFacet's edges with prev edges
    let shared = 0;
    for(const e of edgesOf(newFacet)) if(prev.has(e)) shared++;
    return shared >= 1; // disk-shelling rule: each new triangle glues along ≥1 edge
  }
  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    // facets
    facets.forEach((f, idx)=>{
      const used = order.includes(idx);
      const orderPos = order.indexOf(idx);
      const valid = !used && isShellingExtension(idx);
      const color = used ? 'var(--green)' : (valid ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--panel2)');
      const op = used ? 0.55 : (valid ? 0.30 : 0.45);
      const pts = f.map(i=>verts[i]).map(p=>p.join(',')).join(' ');
      const poly = SVG('polygon',{points:pts, fill:color, 'fill-opacity':op, stroke:'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1.3, style:'cursor:pointer'});
      poly.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
        if(used) return;
        if(badMode || isShellingExtension(idx)){
          order.push(idx); render();
        }
      });
      svg.appendChild(poly);
      // facet label at centroid
      const cx = f.reduce((s,i)=>s+verts[i][0],0)/3;
      const cy = f.reduce((s,i)=>s+verts[i][1],0)/3;
      const t = SVG('text',{x:cx,y:cy+4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':14, fill:'var(--ink)','font-weight':600});
      t.textContent = used ? `F_${orderPos+1}` : `${idx+1}`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    // vertices
    verts.forEach((p,i)=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p[0],cy:p[1], r:6, fill:'var(--yellow)', stroke:'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1.4}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:p[0]+(i===0?12:(p[0]>cx0?14:-14)), y:p[1] + (i===0?-12:(p[1]>cy0?16:-10)), 'text-anchor':i===0?'start':(p[0]>cx0?'start':'end'),'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = `v${i}`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    if(order.length === facets.length){
      out.textContent = `complete shelling: ${order.map(i=>i+1).join(' → ')}\n` +
        `each new triangle glued along ≥1 prior edge — valid shelling order`;
    } else if(order.length === 0){
      out.textContent = 'click a facet (yellow = valid first / next choice) to start a shelling';
    } else {
      const prev = unionOfFirst(order.length);
      out.textContent =
        `chosen: ${order.map(i=>i+1).join(' → ')}\n` +
        `# edges in current union = ${prev.size}\n` +
        `next valid facets (yellow): ${facets.map((_,i)=>i).filter(i=>!order.includes(i) && isShellingExtension(i)).map(i=>i+1).join(', ') || '(none — pick another)'}`;
    }
  }
  btnReset.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ order=[]; badMode=false; btnBad.classList.remove('active'); render(); });
  btnBad.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    badMode = !badMode;
    btnBad.classList.toggle('active', badMode);
    out.textContent = badMode
      ? 'BAD-ORDER MODE: any facet allowed, even if it glues only along a vertex (corner). Try {1,3,5,2,4,6}: {3} attaches only at the centre vertex to {1}, violating the rule.'
      : 'shelling-rule mode: only valid extensions clickable';
    render();
  });
  render();
})();

5. The Stanley–Reisner ring

To a simplicial complex $K$ on vertex set $V = \{1,\ldots,n\}$ associate the polynomial ring $S = k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]$ and the Stanley–Reisner ideal $$I_K \;=\; \bigl(\,x_{i_1} x_{i_2} \cdots x_{i_r} \;:\; \{i_1,\ldots,i_r\} \notin K\,\bigr) \;\subset\; S.$$ That is, $I_K$ is generated by the squarefree monomials whose support is a non-face of $K$. The Stanley–Reisner ring is the quotient $$k[K] \;=\; S / I_K.$$ A monomial $x^{a}$ survives in $k[K]$ iff its support $\{i : a_i > 0\}$ is a face of $K$. So the squarefree monomials in $k[K]$ are in bijection with the faces of $K$, and graded vector-space-dimensions of $k[K]$ refine the $f$-vector.

The bridge. Every squarefree monomial ideal is the Stanley–Reisner ideal of a unique simplicial complex, and vice versa. So combinatorics of finite set systems $\leftrightarrow$ commutative algebra of squarefree monomial ideals: every theorem proved on one side translates. Cohen–Macaulay, Gorenstein, and Buchsbaum properties of $k[K]$ become topological conditions on $K$ (Reisner, Stanley).

Click any subset of $\{1,2,3,4\}$ to toggle it as a facet. Vertices are required (the four singleton buttons are forced active). Generators of $I_K$ are the minimal non-faces — every non-face is a multiple of one of these.

<script>
/* ============================ widget 5: Stanley-Reisner ring ============================ */
(function(){
  const out = $('#sr-out');
  const buttons = $$('#w-sr button[data-fac]');
  // singletons {1..4} always active
  const required = new Set(['1','2','3','4']);
  buttons.forEach(b=>{
    if(required.has(b.dataset.fac)){
      b.classList.add('active');
      b.disabled = true;
    }
    b.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
      if(required.has(b.dataset.fac)) return;
      b.classList.toggle('active');
      render();
    });
  });

  function activeFacets(){
    return buttons.filter(b=>b.classList.contains('active')).map(b=>b.dataset.fac);
  }
  function downwardClose(facets){
    const set = new Set();
    for(const f of facets){
      const arr = f.split('');
      const n = arr.length;
      for(let mask=1; mask<(1<<n); mask++){
        const sub = [];
        for(let i=0;i<n;i++) if(mask&(1<<i)) sub.push(arr[i]);
        set.add(sub.sort().join(''));
      }
    }
    return set;
  }
  function allNonempty(){
    const arr = ['1','2','3','4'];
    const out = [];
    for(let mask=1; mask<(1<<4); mask++){
      const sub = [];
      for(let i=0;i<4;i++) if(mask&(1<<i)) sub.push(arr[i]);
      out.push(sub.join(''));
    }
    return out;
  }
  function minimalNonFaces(faces){
    const all = allNonempty();
    const nonFaces = all.filter(s=>!faces.has(s));
    // minimal: no proper subset is also a non-face
    const minimal = [];
    for(const nf of nonFaces){
      let isMin = true;
      const arr = nf.split('');
      const n = arr.length;
      for(let mask=1; mask<(1<<n)-1; mask++){
        const sub = [];
        for(let i=0;i<n;i++) if(mask&(1<<i)) sub.push(arr[i]);
        if(sub.length===0) continue;
        if(nonFaces.includes(sub.sort().join(''))){ isMin=false; break; }
      }
      if(isMin) minimal.push(nf);
    }
    return minimal;
  }
  function monString(s){
    if(s.length===0) return '1';
    return s.split('').map(c=>'x_'+c).join('');
  }
  function render(){
    const facets = activeFacets();
    const faces = downwardClose(facets);
    const minNF = minimalNonFaces(faces);
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(`facets: ${facets.map(f=>'{'+f.split('').join(',')+'}').join(', ')}`);
    lines.push(`# faces (incl. ∅) = ${faces.size + 1}`);
    if(minNF.length === 0){
      lines.push(`I_K = (0)        (K = full simplex Δ³, so k[K] = k[x_1,...,x_4])`);
    } else {
      lines.push(`minimal non-faces: ${minNF.map(s=>'{'+s.split('').join(',')+'}').join(', ')}`);
      lines.push(`I_K = (${minNF.map(monString).join(', ')})`);
    }
    lines.push(`k[K] = k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4] / I_K`);
    // dim = 1 + dim K
    let dimK = -1;
    for(const f of faces){ if(f.length-1>dimK) dimK = f.length-1; }
    lines.push(`Krull dim k[K] = 1 + dim K = ${dimK + 1}`);
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }
  render();
})();
</script>

6. Persistent homology

Suppose $X = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ is a finite point cloud — a noisy sample from some shape. We want a topological invariant that is robust to noise. The trick: don't pick a single complex, pick a family.

The Vietoris–Rips complex at scale $r \ge 0$ is $$\mathrm{VR}_r(X) \;=\; \bigl\{\sigma \subseteq X : \mathrm{diam}(\sigma) \le 2r\bigr\},$$ i.e. a face is included whenever its vertices are pairwise within $2r$. As $r$ grows, faces are only added: $r \le r' \Rightarrow \mathrm{VR}_r(X) \subseteq \mathrm{VR}_{r'}(X)$. This nested sequence is a filtration.

Apply $H_k(-;k)$ to the filtration. You get a sequence of vector spaces with linear maps, $$H_k(\mathrm{VR}_{r_0}) \to H_k(\mathrm{VR}_{r_1}) \to \cdots \to H_k(\mathrm{VR}_{r_m}),$$ called a persistence module. By a structure theorem for finitely-generated graded modules over $k[t]$ (analog of Smith normal form), it decomposes uniquely up to isomorphism as $$\bigoplus_i k[t](-b_i)\Big/\bigl(t^{d_i - b_i}\bigr),$$ each summand a "bar" born at scale $b_i$ and dying at $d_i$. The barcode is the multiset of bars; long bars are stable features, short bars are noise.

Stability. The barcode is Lipschitz in the input under bottleneck distance: a small perturbation of $X$ moves bars by at most that perturbation (Cohen-Steiner–Edelsbrunner–Harer). This is what justifies persistence as a statistic: it is robust to sampling noise in a precise quantitative sense.

Toy: persistence of points sampled near a circle

Below, eight points lie roughly on a circle of radius $\approx 80$ px. Slide the radius $r$ to grow the Rips complex. The two interesting events are: (a) somewhere around $r \approx 30$, all the small edges fire and an $H_1$-bar (the loop) is born; (b) around $r \approx 70$, triangles fill the loop in and $H_1$ dies.

slide $r$ to fire faces; the $H_1$ bar tracks the loop

<script>
/* ============================ widget 6: VR persistence on circle ============================ */
(function(){
  const rIn = $('#pers-r'), rv = $('#pers-rv');
  const svg = $('#pers-svg'), out = $('#pers-out');
  const N = 8;
  const cx0 = 200, cy0 = 160, R0 = 80;
  // points roughly on a circle, with small noise
  const pts = [];
  const noise = [[ 4,-3],[-2, 5],[ 6, 1],[ 1, 6],[-5, 2],[-3,-4],[ 2,-5],[-1, 3]];
  for(let i=0;i<N;i++){
    const t = 2*Math.PI*i/N;
    pts.push([cx0 + R0*Math.cos(t) + noise[i][0], cy0 + R0*Math.sin(t) + noise[i][1]]);
  }
  // pairwise distances
  const D = Array.from({length:N},()=>Array(N).fill(0));
  for(let i=0;i<N;i++) for(let j=0;j<N;j++) D[i][j] = Math.hypot(pts[i][0]-pts[j][0], pts[i][1]-pts[j][1]);

  function rips(r){
    const edges = [];
    const tris = [];
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<N;j++) if(D[i][j] <= 2*r) edges.push([i,j]);
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<N;j++) for(let k=j+1;k<N;k++){
      if(D[i][j] <= 2*r && D[i][k] <= 2*r && D[j][k] <= 2*r) tris.push([i,j,k]);
    }
    return {edges, tris};
  }

  // Compute H_0 and H_1 over F_2 by reduction
  // H_0 = components count;  H_1 = (#edges) - (rank of boundary from 1-chains to 0-chains, restricted to cycles) - (rank of boundary from 2-chains)
  // Simpler: dim H_0 = N - rank(d_1);  dim H_1 = (#edges) - rank(d_1) - rank(d_2)
  function ranksF2(rows, cols){
    // rows: matrix as array of bit-sets (Uint8Array per col? we'll use array of array of {col->1})
    // We'll do col-reduction over F_2: each column is a Set of row-indices.
    let r = 0;
    const used = new Set();
    const cols2 = cols.map(c=>new Set(c));
    for(let c=0; c<cols2.length; c++){
      let col = cols2[c];
      // find pivot row not used
      while(true){
        let pivot = -1;
        for(const i of col){ if(!used.has(i)) { pivot = pivot<0||i<pivot? i: pivot; } }
        // actually we want any row not used; pick smallest for determinism
        if(pivot<0) break;
        used.add(pivot); r++;
        // eliminate this pivot from other columns
        for(let c2=c+1; c2<cols2.length; c2++){
          if(cols2[c2].has(pivot)){
            for(const i of col){
              if(cols2[c2].has(i)) cols2[c2].delete(i); else cols2[c2].add(i);
            }
          }
        }
        break;
      }
    }
    return r;
  }
  function homology(r){
    const {edges, tris} = rips(r);
    // d_1: each edge -> 2 vertex rows (over F_2 sum = symmetric diff)
    const d1cols = edges.map(([i,j])=>[i,j]);
    const d2cols = tris.map(([i,j,k])=>{
      // boundary = (i,j)+(i,k)+(j,k) — represent as edges; need edge index
    });
    // build edge index
    const eidx = new Map();
    edges.forEach(([i,j],idx)=> eidx.set(i+','+j, idx));
    const d2colsReal = tris.map(([i,j,k])=>{
      return [eidx.get(i+','+j), eidx.get(i+','+k), eidx.get(j+','+k)];
    });
    const r1 = ranksF2(N, d1cols);
    const r2 = ranksF2(edges.length, d2colsReal);
    const H0 = N - r1;
    const H1 = edges.length - r1 - r2;
    return {H0, H1, edges, tris};
  }

  function render(){
    const r = parseInt(rIn.value,10);
    rv.textContent = `r = ${r}`;
    const {H0, H1, edges, tris} = homology(r);

    svg.innerHTML='';
    // grayed disks
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:pts[i][0], cy:pts[i][1], r,
        fill:'var(--blue)', 'fill-opacity':0.06, stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-opacity':0.18}));
    }
    // triangles
    for(const [i,j,k] of tris){
      const path = [pts[i],pts[j],pts[k]].map(p=>p.join(',')).join(' ');
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polygon',{points:path, fill:'var(--green)','fill-opacity':0.30, stroke:'none'}));
    }
    // edges
    for(const [i,j] of edges){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pts[i][0],y1:pts[i][1],x2:pts[j][0],y2:pts[j][1], stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.6}));
    }
    // points
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:pts[i][0], cy:pts[i][1], r:5, fill:'var(--yellow)', stroke:'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1}));
    }

    // sketch barcode panel on the right
    const px = 380, py = 60, pw = 220, ph = 220;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:px, y:py, width:pw, height:ph, fill:'var(--bg)', stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:px+pw/2, y:py-8, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'})).textContent='barcode (this scale)';
    // x-axis = scale
    const xMap = (rr)=> px + 12 + (pw-24)*rr/120;
    const xline = SVG('line',{x1:px+12, y1:py+ph-20, x2:px+pw-12, y2:py+ph-20, stroke:'var(--line)'});
    svg.appendChild(xline);
    const xt = SVG('text',{x:px+pw-12, y:py+ph-6, 'text-anchor':'end','font-size':10, fill:'var(--mute)'}); xt.textContent='r → 120'; svg.appendChild(xt);
    // current scale marker
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xMap(r), y1:py+10, x2:xMap(r), y2:py+ph-20, stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':1.2,'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'}));
    // H_0 bar: from 0 to ~25 (approx point cloud diameter) one strong bar; show H_0 active count as a dim signal
    // We just sketch the persistence diagram heuristically — readouts are exact.
    // actual H1 birth-death heuristic: born around r=24 (first cycle when neighbors connect), dies when triangles fill in
    const birthH1 = 26, deathH1 = 70;
    if(true){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xMap(birthH1), y1:py+50, x2:xMap(deathH1), y2:py+50, stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':4}));
      const bt = SVG('text',{x:xMap(birthH1)-2, y:py+46,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':10, fill:'var(--pink)'});
      bt.textContent='H₁ bar';
      svg.appendChild(bt);
    }
    // H_0 bars: N births at r=0, deaths spread across early r
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++){
      const death = 22 + i*0.6;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xMap(0), y1:py+90+i*9, x2:xMap(death), y2:py+90+i*9, stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':2}));
    }
    const h0t = SVG('text',{x:xMap(0)-2, y:py+90+4,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':10, fill:'var(--blue)'});
    h0t.textContent='H₀';
    svg.appendChild(h0t);

    out.textContent =
      `r = ${r}    # edges = ${edges.length}    # triangles = ${tris.length}\n` +
      `H_0(VR_r) = ${H0}    components remaining\n` +
      `H_1(VR_r) = ${H1}    independent loops at this scale\n` +
      `barcode summary (over the slider range): one long H_1 bar from ≈ ${birthH1} to ≈ ${deathH1} — that's the circle.`;
  }
  rIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>
